coredata.dc:title: A multi-channel array for metal ions discrimination with animal bones derived biomass carbon dots as sensing units
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Lixing
Liu, Ting
Yang, Fan
Wu, Meng
Yin, Chenhui
Chen, Ligang
Niu, Na
coredata.dc:description: This paper designed a multivariate analysis method through carbon dots (CDs) to detect and distinguish heavy metal ions in aqueous systems. The carbon dots were derived from three kinds of animal bones through hydrothermal process. Therefore, N, O, S, Ca elements contained in the three different bones themselves are doped directly into the carbon dots by hydrothermal method. CDs gained from diverse raw materials (pork bone, bovine bone, and sheep bone) interact with heavy metal ions of varying degrees were used as probes to distinguish heavy metal ions Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+. Specific fingerprints are formed with the output signal, which is the changing rate of fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of metal ions to the bone CDs. The fingerprints indicate Fe3+ has the highest quenching effect on three biomass CDs, while Ag+ has the best quenching effect on sheep bone biomass CD and the detection range was 40–4000 nM. Hierarchical cluster analysis and linear discriminant analysis were used to distinguish Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+. The accuracy is 100% not only for individual ions, but also for binary and ternary mixtures. Simultaneously, metal ions in environmental water have been successfully distinguished. The sensor array can identify various metal ions in water simply, efficiently and systematically at the same time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor array
Biomass carbon dots
Self-doping
Metal ions
Linear discriminant analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The process of detecting metal ions based on BCDs
Application of the sensor array to water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of bone biomass carbon dots and discrimination of five metal ions based on multi-channel BCDs array.
TEM images of (a) PBCDs, (b) BBCDs and (c) SBCDs.
(a) XRD (b) FT-IR (c) UV–vis spectra of biomass CDs.
Fluorescence responses (F-F0) /F0 of five metals to PBCD (a); BBCD (b); SBCD (c) at Ex = 350 nm.

Response fingerprint at Ex = 350 nm of three BCDs to five metal ions at pH = 6 (a); pH = 7 (b); pH = 8 (c) and canonical score plot for the response patterns as obtained from LDA for five heavy metal ions at pH = 6 (d); pH = 7 (e); pH = 8 (f) and HCA tree graph of three BCDs to five metal ions at pH = 6 (g); pH = 7 (h); pH = 8 (i).
Canonical score plot for the response patterns as obtained from LDA for five heavy metal ions at 80 (a), 160 (b), 500 (c), 1000 (d), 2000 (e), 4000 (f) nM at Ex = 350 nm.
(a) Fingerprint response at Ex = 350 nm; (b) the score scattering plots obtained by LDA; (c) HCA tree graph of mixture metal ions to three BCDs.
Response fingerprint at Ex = 350 nm (a); and LDA plot (b) of three BCDs to five metal ions with environment water at 40 nM.
Comparison of different methods for the discrimination to metal ions.

row.entry: Ti4+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb4+, Sn4+
River, paint, rain near industrial areas

head.ce:title: A multi-channel array for metal ions discrimination with animal bones derived biomass carbon dots as sensing units

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper designed a multivariate analysis method through carbon dots (CDs) to detect and distinguish heavy metal ions in aqueous systems. The carbon dots were derived from three kinds of animal bones through hydrothermal process. Therefore, N, O, S, Ca elements contained in the three different bones themselves are doped directly into the carbon dots by hydrothermal method. CDs gained from diverse raw materials (pork bone, bovine bone, and sheep bone) interact with heavy metal ions of varying degrees were used as probes to distinguish heavy metal ions Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+. Specific fingerprints are formed with the output signal, which is the changing rate of fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of metal ions to the bone CDs. The fingerprints indicate Fe3+ has the highest quenching effect on three biomass CDs, while Ag+ has the best quenching effect on sheep bone biomass CD and the detection range was 40–4000 nM. Hierarchical cluster analysis and linear discriminant analysis were used to distinguish Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+. The accuracy is 100% not only for individual ions, but also for binary and ternary mixtures. Simultaneously, metal ions in environmental water have been successfully distinguished. The sensor array can identify various metal ions in water simply, efficiently and systematically at the same time.

ce:section.ce:para: The developing brain would be severely damaged owing to the toxic accumulation of heavy metals in the soft tissues of human body. In the process of metal mining purification, acid mine drainage, industrial effluent discharge, etc, the ecological environment and human beings are directly or indirectly polluted through bioaccumulation. The problems of complex program setting, expensive equipment and unfavorable field analysis are the obstacles of atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [1] and inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [2] in the direction of quantitative detection of metal ions. Low cost, simple and sensitive metal ion detection methods has become the most important research work.

Optical detection method, especially fluorescence method, has the characteristics of high sensitivity, simple process and low cost, which just offsets the application gap of large-scale instrument detection mentioned above. Carbon Dots (CDs) is an excellent carbon fluorescent nano-material with chemical stability and superior biocompatibility [3]. First of all, a variety of metal ions can interact with CDs. There are a large number of groups on the surface of the CDs, such as amino, carboxyl groups, so that non-specific sensing can be achieved [4]. Secondly, the types and quantities of groups can also be shaped by changing raw materials, synthesis methods, induction conditions, etc. For example, the CDs synthesized by microwave, hydrothermal and plasma treatment with same materials have distinctive responses to varied metal ions [5]. Finally, the synthetic materials of CDs not only can be chemistry reagent [6], but also can be widely presented in nature and daily life, such as garlic skins [7], crab shells [8], mango skins [9], Poa pratensis [10], harmful diesel soot [11] and kerosene soot [12]. By doping other elements (such as sulfur, nitrogen and phosphorus), CDs can be customized with novel surface states and special characteristics, such as pH sensitivity [13] and high quantum yield [14]. Wang's team [15] have developed a S, N co-doped CD with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 73.1%, which has good acid resistance, alkali resistance, salt stability, photostability and photobleaching resistance.

However, according to the traditional sensor mode, it is reported in the review [16] the detection of a series of metal ions requires the synthesis of sensing elements with specific response to each metal ion, which is difficult to synthesize. At the same time, detection of multiple metal ions often requires exact sensors to work separately [17], which is expensive and time-consuming. The sensor array solves the problem of detecting a series of metal ions in one way, while simultaneously detecting a mixture of ions. The sensing platform is described as an anthropomorphic electronic tongue [18], a bionic system designed to mimic the mammalian taste system. In an electronic tongue system, each sensing unit responds to a series of samples semi-selectively with generating specific fingerprint. The formation of cross reaction is the core of sensor array system design. At present, several sensor arrays for metal ions recognition have been reported, such as, Lin's group [19] had developed a multi-channel fluorescent sensor array based on organically synthesized small molecules for metal ions recognition, Li's team [20] detected metal ions through the aggregation behavior of various gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) that produce different colorimetric response patterns. But there are still some problems of complex synthesis, high cost and so on. As a sensor array, its sensitiveness and perception capacity depend on the number of sensing elements, which leads us to find new materials with further modification and doping.

In this study, carbon dots were composed by animal bones, pyrolyzed, oxidated by H2SO4 and hydrothermally treated eventually. The hydrothermal synthesis of tea has been used to make CDs [21], yet, the quantum yield is only about 4.9%, due to only N doped. Bones contain a lot of Ca, N, and S elements, which make the heteroatoms dope into the CDs without adding reagents. Realizing the self-doping of Ca, N, and S elements, bones can improve the quantum yield of CDs compared with only N-doping. In addition, bones as a kind of kitchen waste will be discarded. Here, these low value wastes are converted into high value CDs to realize the reuse of waste resources. The obtained biomass carbon dots (BCDs) have excellent optical properties, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Pork bone BCDs (PBCDs), bovine bone BCDs (BBCDs) and sheep bone BCDs (SBCDs) react with metal ions can produce different fluorescence responses, which will generate fingerprints for given analytes. A multipass fluorescence sensor array based on BCDs for accurately identifying Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+ has been developed (Fig. 1 ). The three fluorescence sensors exhibit different affinity with Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+, distinct fluorescence responses to each metal ion were obtained jointly. Each Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+, as well as mixtures of binary and multivariate metal ions, was accurately identified by using multi-channel signals combined with linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and principal component analysis (PCA). The results show that the array can quickly and simultaneously identify a variety of metal ions, adding color to the research process of multi-target recognition.

The bones from sheep, pork and bovine used to prepare the CDs came from a local supermarket (Harbin, China). The metal salts including FeCl3, CuCl2, Pb(CH3COO)2 and H2SO4, were purchased by Kermel (Tianjin, China). AgNO3 was bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). HgCl2 was bought from Jiangyan Global Reagent Factory (Jiangyan, China). All the chemical grade is analytical grade. The water used to prepare the solution is deionized water. The environmental water comes from Harbin (China).
All instruments used in this experiment are listed in the Electronic Supporting Material (ESM).

The meat and other impurities remaining on the bovine bones were cleaned with deionized water. The processed bones were dried at 55℃ for 10 h, then crushed with a shredder. Bovine bones (4.0 g) were placed in muffle oven and heated at 700℃ for 5 h. Grind the carbonized powder thoroughly, weigh 1.0 g and disperse it in 3 mL water and 10 mL sulfuric acid (98.3%), sonicate for 20 min. After centrifugally washing to neutral, the powder was placed in a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 200℃ for 10 h. Finally, it was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane.
The preparation methods of BCDs with sheep and bovine bones as carbon source were done as above, but the pork bone was replaced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The process of detecting metal ions based on BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The BCDs solutions and a series of Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ solutions with different concentrations were added into a cuvette. The concentrations of metal ions were 40, 80, 160, 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 nM, respectively. Disodium hydrogen phosphate-sodium dihydrogen phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) was used in this system. The volumes of BCDs, metal ions and buffer solutions are 0.5 mL, 1 mL and 1 mL. After incubation for 5 min, the solution was analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy at 350 nm excitation wavelength. F and F0 were recorded as the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of metal ions respectively.

Binary mixtures of Ag+/Fe3+, Cu2+/Fe3+, Ag+/Cu2+ were mixed with total concentration at 40 nM. The detection procedures were same as single metal ion. Ternary mixture of Ag+/Fe3+/Cu2+ was prepared with equal volume at 40 nM total concentration. The detection process is same as above.

The environmental water sample may cause changes in the fluorescence response pattern, so the water sample is filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. The metal ions Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+ prepared with environment water were added to the BCDs. The volume of solutions and measurement were same as above.

Fingerprints were used to construct sensor array. Five repetitions were generated for each detection procedure and training data matrixes with 3 BCDs × 5 metal ions × 5 replicates have been produced. The obtained four-mixtures data matrix is the same as above.

In this study, a bottom-up method was adopted to prepare fluorescent BCDs with bone wastes as carbon source by combining high temperature pyrolysis with concentrated H2SO4 oxidation assisted hydrothermal method. The high concentration of H2SO4 oxidized precursor bone enriched the BCDs functional surface groups. The acid treatment (H2SO4) process mainly used the oxidation etching effect of strong acid to etch the internal structure of bones and make its surface oxidized. At the same time, the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups made the BCDs have excellent hydrophilic properties and the surface easier modify. The hydrothermal reaction temperature and time are important factors affecting the fluorescence properties of BCDs. The effects of different hydrothermal reaction temperatures on the fluorescence intensity of BCDs were investigated. With the temperature increasing from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually. This may be the effect of temperature on the degree of bone carbonization. The higher the temperature, the more surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups are likely to form on the surface of BCDs [22]. When the temperature is in the range of 200–220 ℃, the increase of fluorescence intensity becomes slowly, as in Fig. S1(a). The number of repetitions was three. During the experiment, high temperature may increase the risk of the experiment due to the limited heat resistance of the reactor. Therefore, 200 ℃ was selected as the optimum reaction temperature. In addition, we also studied the effect of different hydrothermal reaction times (6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h) on the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. The fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the reaction time, as shown in Fig. S1(b). The number of repetitions was three. However, the increase of reaction time at 10 and 12 h was not significant. Therefore, the hydrothermal reaction conditions for the preparation of BCDs were set at 200 ℃ for 10 h.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM spectrum of BCDs. Three kinds of biomass CDs are evenly dispersed and have distinct boundaries. It can be seen from the figure that the shape of the BCDs is elliptical or circular. The average diameters of PBCDs, BBCDs and SBCDs are 6.7 nm, 6.6 nm and 9.5 nm, respectively. HRTEM images (illustrated in Fig. 2) and SAED images (Fig. S2) showed that biomass CDs had clear lattice structures with lattice spacing of 0.13 nm, 0.11 nm and 0.12 nm respectively.

XRD pattern of common CDs have a wide diffraction peak in the range of 20°–25° [23], which is different from synthetic BCDs, there are obvious diffraction peaks at 11.7°, 20.8°, 23.4° and 29.1°, as Fig. 3 (a) shows. This phenomenon indicates the formation of an ordered highly crystalline structure in the BCDs, which is consistent with the TEM results. It is speculated that the reason for the formation of different structures is that the self-doping of elements helps to promote the ordered structure of BCDs and a higher macroscopic crystallinity. At the same time, the large amount of nitrogen in bones promotes the formation of polycyclic fragments in BCDs structure.

Fig. 3(b) has shown the surface functional groups of three BCDs characterized by FT-IR. The stretching vibration of N–H and O–H can be seen at the peak of 3439 cm−1, and –C–H vibration from 2937 cm−1. C-N and S-H groups have weak absorption peak at 2365 cm−1. Location of 1426 cm−1, 1569 cm−1 are characteristic peaks respectively attributed to the presence of C–NH–C and C=C stretching vibration [24] in BCDs. The characteristic absorption peaks of C–N, C–O and C–S groups are 1131 cm−1 [25].

Fig. 3(c) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BCDs. It can be seen from the figure that the absorption of BCDs in the UV–vis region is obvious, but the absorption in visible light area is relatively weak. Their characteristic peaks are around 259 nm, which caused by π - π * transition of C=C.

Taking SBCDs as an example, the XPS spectra of the three BCDs are basically the same. The XPS full spectrum scan of SBCD showed five main signal peaks, as shown in Fig. S3(a), indicating the presence of C, Ca, O, N, and S elements. The element contents of XPS analysis results are shown in Table S1. The different response of metal ions to three BCDs also stems from the distinction in element content. The XPS spectrum of PBCD and BBCD are in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, respectively. The XPS high-resolution map of C1s is shown in Fig. S3(b), the peak at 284.0, 284.5, 284.9, 285.4 eV hinted the presence of C–C/C=C, C–S, C–N and C–O groups. The fitting crests at 530.2, 530.9 and 531.8 eV show the existence of C=O, C–O–C and C–O–H groups (Fig. S3(c)). There are two characteristic peaks in Ca2p spectrum (Fig. S3(d)). The presence of Ca2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2 is responsible for the production of 346.8 eV and 350.2 eV peaks. The high resolution energy spectrum of N1s (Fig. S3(e)) shows the existence of C–N–C, N–(C)3 and N–H groups, respectively located at 398.1, 399.5, 400.8 eV. There are two fitting peaks in S2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S3(f)), which are located at 168.2 and 169.7 eV, due to the existence of -C-SO4- and -C-SO3-. The five orbital electrons in the outermost layer of nitrogen atom can bond stably with carbon atom, while the higher emission trap density of sulfur atom can correct the energy gap, and the doping of N and S can effectively enhance the fluorescence properties [26].

When the excitation wavelength of BCDs is within 310–400 nm, the emission peak of BCDs is basically constant at 453 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength, which indicates that the three prepared BCDs have no excitation dependence (Fig. S6). The fluorescence signal with the highest fluorescence intensity appeared at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Previously, researchers have reported [27] that BCDs have excitation independent characteristics, but they all need to be purified by silica column. However, the BCDs prepared in this paper have no excitation dependence without further purification. Generally, the excitation dependence of BCDs is mainly caused by different surface states and particle size. We speculate that the surface defects of BCDs are passivated by N–Ca–N, C–N, C=O, C–O and other functional groups. The excitation independent emission characteristic is due to that only the single mode radiation transition of carbon sp2 can generate the fluorescence emission. In addition, the quantum yields (QY) of PBCD, BBCD, SBCD were 7.3%, 8.4%, 8.0% by using quinine sulfate as relative standard in reference [28], the formula is listed in supplementary material.

For metal salt, anion is an important component, which will affect the detection results. CO3 2–, SO4 2−, F−, NO3 –, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, Cl− solutions (with the concentration of 4000 nM) were added into BCDs. The fluorescence spectra of BCDs after adding different anions were shown in Fig. S7. It was noted that there was no obvious optical change in fluorescence intensity of BCDs after the addition of anions, indicating anions don’t interfere with detection.

It is very important to determine the pH value for the detection of metal ions in BCDs system [29,30]. The effect of pH (3–12) has been discussed. It can be seen from Fig. S8 that the fluorescence intensity of BCDs decreased sharply under acidic conditions (pH 3–6) and extremely alkaline conditions (pH 12), but remained unchanged at the pH range of 7–11. Therefore, we explored the effects of pH (7–11) on metal ions detection system, as Fig. S9 shown. After the addition of metal ions, the fluorescence intensity decreased, and the decrease reached the maximum at pH 7.

During the establishment of the database, their test environment remains consistent. Therefore, the differential response can only be caused by the BCDs and the metal ion themselves. Due to different synthetic raw materials, BCDs have differences in element content, which is also reflected in the characterization results of XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis, XPS, spectra and TEM of BCDs and so on. Each metal ion responds differently to the BCDs because of its different orbital and electron motion. Each metal ion has a differentiated response to each BCD, and a three-dimensional fingerprint of the metal ion is obtained.

The quenching behavior of five metal ions ranges from 40 nM to 4000 nM. Fluorescence spectra of BCDs before and after adding five metal ions in different concentrations have been related to Fig. S10. As shown in Fig. S10, with the increase of the concentration of five metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of the three BCDs decreased rapidly and significantly. Different metal ions have obvious effects on the quenching degree of the three BCDs and their quenching degrees have been placed in Fig. 4 . When the concentration of Fe3+ is 4000 nM, the quenching degree of the BBCD system gets 78.5%, reaching the upper limit of the system. When Pb2+ is added to PBCD, the lowest limit of analysis, 40 nM, is reached. For the same metal ions, the quenching extent presents a steady upward trend. The quenching degree of Fe3+ to BCDs is obviously stronger than other ions. At the same time, the quenching degree of SBCD is bigger than the other two BCDs. In Fig. 4(b), the quenching of BBCD by Hg2+ and Cu2+ are highly similar, while the introduction of the other two BCDs makes the quenching of BBCD by Hg2+ and Cu2+ reflect the differentiation. The value (F-F0)/F0 reflects the difference of fluorescence response modes of the five metal ions, that is, the feasibility of this method to identify metal ions.

Three BCDs solutions were mixed with 40 nM Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+ respectively, and repeated measurements were made for 5 times. A multi-dimensional response mode with 3 sensing units × 5 metals × 5 repeated was obtained. The fingerprints of each metal ion are generated according to the change of fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 5 (b). When metal ions were added to the BCDs, the fluorescence emission wavelength did not shift. The difference of fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of metal ion solution was normalized to obtain D value (=(F-F0)/F0), which constituted the fingerprint. Once the binding characteristics of BCDs and five metal ions have been established, it can construct a sensor array composed of BBCD, SBCD and PBCD as sensing units for metal ion sensing. LDA [31], a qualitative statistical method widely used in pattern recognition, is used to analyze the multiple response patterns of the sensor array in the addition of Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+ for statistical analysis. LDA is used to illustrate the distinguishing ability of the sensor system that attempts to find a linear combination of several kinds of event features to distinguish them as a linear classifier.

The three typical factors generated by LDA in this sensing system represent the extent to cover all the information. The variance contribution rates are 77.7%, 18.8% and 3.5% respectively. The first two factors can cover 96.5% of the overall information. As shown in LDA canonical score plot for the response patterns (Fig. 5(e)), twenty-five training data sets are divided into 5 groups, each point represents the response mode of a single metal ion sample to the sensor array. The subset analysis of the data set demonstrated the accuracy of the classification matrix, which is 100%. The results of HCA to metal ions at 40 nM are shown in Fig. 5(h). As a multivariate statistical analysis method, HCA [32] consists of aggregation and splitting methods and can find observed clusters in data sets [33]. Even in the absence of prior knowledge, reasonable clustering can be performed according to the characteristics of data. HCA can perfectly classify all D values into the same group, and at the same time, different metal ions can be accurately classified into five different categories. All the metal ions at various concentrations can be classified with 100% accuracy in the LDA scatter plot, as shown in Fig. 6 .

It can be learned that the fluorescence response fingerprints of three BCDs to five ions at different pH from Fig. 5(a–c). Disodium hydrogen phosphate-sodium dihydrogen phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6, 7, 8) was used in this system. In the process of pH change, three BCDs and five ions have different optical responses. Five metal ions can be correctly classified by LDA (Fig. 5(d–f)) at different pH. However, according to the HCA diagram (Fig. 5(g–i)), when pH = 6 and pH = 8, Ag+ and Pb2 + gather in one layer, which can’t be effectively distinguished. Therefore, pH = 7 is the best experimental condition. It is speculated that the preparative BCDs can form stable conjugated structure under neutral condition, so it has different responses to ions. When BCDs solutions are in other pH conditions, on account of the variation or disruption of specific conjugation structure, the response of metal ions to BCDs can’t form a specific linear combination, which can’t distinguish metal ions.

It is a challenge for sensor array system to distinguish different targets in the hybrid system. LDA can be used to “train” known samples and identify unknown analytes, which is a supervised method. The new case is assigned to the group with the shortest Mahalanobis distance (the relative distance of the centroid of each training sample group in multidimensional space). At the same time, two or more analytes can be separated by the linear combination of features. Four binary and ternary mixtures, Ag+/Fe3+, Cu2+/Fe3+, Ag+/Cu2+, Ag+/Fe3+/Cu2+, with the concentration ratio of 1:1 and the total concentration of 40 nM, were also analyzed by LDA. The fluorescence response fingerprint of the mixture and the LDA fractional scatter diagram are shown in Fig. 7 (a) and Fig. 7(b) respectively. In the diagram, different mixed separators can be scattered, and the same mixed separators can be clustered. The accuracy is 100%. In the HCA tree (Fig. 7(c)), the clusters of different mixed ions can be divided into four clusters and finally converge into one cluster.

After the analytes are absorbed by the lattice of BCDs, the change of intermolecular interaction (such as weak metal to metal interactions, π accumulation, hydrogen bonds, and C–H-π interactions) have generated. The interaction results in the change of fluorescence intensity. Coordinated interactions between hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups trigger to fluorescence quenching [34]. The fluorescence lifetime test was carried out to explore the quenching mechanism of metal ions on BCDs, as shown in Fig. S11. The quenching of BBCD by Pb2+ is illustrated as an example. According to Fig. S11(b) and (h), the fluorescence lifetime of BBCDs with and without metal ions is almost unchanged, 5.272 ns and 5.25041 ns, respectively, so it is speculated that the quenching mechanism is static quenching [35]. To further explore the quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption tests of BCDs, metal ions and different metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+) at different concentrations (40, 160, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 nM) added into BCDs have been shown in Fig. S12. For the convenience of comparison, normalization treatment was carried out. The quenching of BBCD by Pb2+ is illustrated. The UV–vis spectra of BBCD indicates two obvious absorption peaks near 280 nm and 350 nm. After the addition of Pb2+, the absorption peaks at 350 nm disappear obviously, and the positions of the absorption peaks at 280 nm shift to the left. These indicate the formation of a new ground state complex. With the increase of Pb2+ concentration, the position of the absorption peak does not change, but the absorbance increases with the increase of Pb2+ concentration. It indicates the formation of a new substance which preliminarily proves that the quenching mechanism is static quenching. In order to confirm the formation of the ground state complex, zeta potential is given in Table S2. Because of a lot of -COOHs exposed on the BCDs surface, the ζ potential of BCDs was measured to be −4.76 mV. In the presence of Pb2+, the ζ potential of BBCDs increased to 0.335 mV. When –COOH and Pb2+ coordinate to form ground state complex, the ζ potential increases and positive charge is obtained, which proves that the quenching mechanism is static quenching. After adding different metal ions into BCDs, the excited states of CDs tend to be stable to different degrees and the spin–orbit coupling is enhanced to different degrees. That’s the reason why the differential approach can be used for five metal ion detection of the three BCDs [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor array to water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of metal ions affects the environmental water quality. According to the results obtained, the system was able to recognize metal ions in environmental water samples to further verify its practical feasibility. No metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+) were detected in the river by ICP. The solutions of Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+ were added to the water samples, and then gained the fluorescence response fingerprint of metal ions showed in Fig. 8 (a). It can be seen from the figure that each metal ion has different quenching degree for three biomass CDs. LDA (Fig. 8(b)) scatter diagram shows that the five metal ions are clearly divided into five categories. The sum of factor 1 and 2 is 99.8%, indicating that it covers most of the sample information and can represent the overall sample. The results confirm the practicability and stability of the sensor array system, and we can accurately identify metal ions with a recognition accuracy of 100%.

Besides, there are also other methods for detecting metal ions, as listed in Table 1 , which contains the sensing units of other methods, detection means, actual samples tested, detection range and types of metal ions. Li's [20] and Tang's [36] team generated different colorimetric response patterns through the aggregation behavior of various gold nanoparticles. By analyzing these response patterns with LDA, the sensor array is able to accurately identify a variety of metal ions, but gold nanoparticles are expensive to synthesize. Based on a wettability conversion matrix [37] composed of cobalt hydroxide nanowidles and azimidazole, the change of contact angle is due to the coordination reaction between the analyte ions and the responder compound, which enables the effective detection of metal ions. However, synthesis is complex and requires molecular derivatization and conformational manipulation of the responder compounds to form the sensing unit. Lee [38] et al. reported that conjugated polymer papers with different physical and chemical properties were prepared by applying different potential to them, which contributed to different response modes and adsorption efficiencies for single metal ion. Pooja's team [39] and Xie's team [40] respectively synthesized functional CdTe QDs to detect metal ions, but the synthesis process also used metal ions, which is highly toxic. Carbon nanomaterials [41] produced from cow dung, straw and sorghum stalks have also been reported to detect metal ions. In terms of the response time, it is 5 min in our paper, meanwhile 5, 10 min [20,36,37,40] have been reported, which means our response time is fast. Compared with these methods, the fluorescence array detection method based on BCDs constructed in this experiment is the same or better at detection range and other aspects, which can distinguish metal ions rapidly and accurately. Introduction of CDs simplifies the synthesis steps of the sensing unit. The use of bone waste as carbon source greatly reduces the preparation cost and realizes the recycling of resources. What’s more, the self-doping of N, S, Ca elements in bones has eliminated the use of reagents. In a word, this method has the advantages of rapidity, good stability, easy repetition, precise discrimination, has great application potential. Although the sensor array can simultaneously realize the identification of multiple objects, it is deficient in quantitative detection somehow. Therefore, in the future work, we will devote ourselves to the qualitative and quantitative analysis of various target objects simultaneously.

In conclusion, we exploited a BCDs fluorescent sensor array with three kinds of bone waste as carbon source to precisely recognize and actively detect metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+). Due to the varied element content of three BCDs, the affinity of CDs to metal ions is different, resulting in diverse quenching degrees. The output of the various signals arises from the synergistic action of the 3D channel sensors, which are the basis for simulating the human taste system to discriminate various metal ions. The highest and lowest points of detection range are generated by Fe3+ to BBCD and Pb2+ to PBCD respectively. LDA and HCA were 100% correct in identifying metal ions Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+, as well as their mixtures. At the same time, the sensor array system is applied to the detection of metal ions in environmental water samples, in which the five kinds of metal ions were distinguished successfully. This provides a strong technical support for the field of environmental monitoring and protection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU03), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team) Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology (Harbin Engineering University).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113638.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lixing Fu is a graduate student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent sensor array and their practical applications. Her contributions are conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, validation, visualization, writing - original draft.

Ting Liu is a graduate student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications. Her contributions are visualization, investigation.

Fan Yang is an undergraduate student at the School of Chemistry and Materials at Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology. His research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications. His contributions are formal analysis, investigation.

Meng Wu is a graduate student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications. Her contributions are formal analysis, investigation.

Chenhui Yin is a graduate student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications. His contributions are formal analysis, investigation.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are fluorescence probes and molecular imprinting techniques. His contributions are supervision, writing - review & editing, funding acquisition.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications. Her contributions are supervision, writing - review & editing, funding acquisition.
